phases.name: Phase 1 · Pre-Match Ritual
phases.title: Pre-Match Research
phases.sub: Complete 30–60 min before first ball. Know both teams, the pitch & conditions cold.

items.q: Who are the openers? Aggressive or anchor types?
items.d: Aggressive = attack from ball 1. Anchor = stabilise then accelerate. Sets the powerplay tone entirely.

items.q: Middle order (3–5) — consolidators or attackers?
items.d: Do they rebuild after early wickets or are they floaters who attack in overs 8–15? Note preferred shots.

items.q: Finishers (6–7) — strike rate above 150 in death overs?
items.d: A strong finisher vs a weak one can be a 20–30 run difference in the last 4 overs. Compare both teams.

items.q: Form of top 3 batters — last 5 innings scores
items.d: Cold batters under pressure push too hard and gift wickets early. In-form batters are match-winners.

items.q: Known weaknesses — short ball, left-arm spin, googly?
items.d: Great bowlers exploit these relentlessly. Know the weakness so you can predict the bowling matchup.

items.q: Confirmed playing XI — any surprise absences?
items.d: A missing key batter fundamentally changes depth. Verify 30 min before toss.

items.q: Fast bowlers vs spinners — how many of each and why?
items.d: Spinning pitch = 2–3 spinners. Seaming pitch = 3–4 fast bowlers. A mismatch signals a gamble.

items.q: Powerplay bowlers (Overs 1–6) — who takes the new ball?
items.d: Your most attacking bowler. Can they swing it early? Early wickets = momentum. Note economy too.

items.q: Middle-over spinners — wicket-taker or stopper?
items.d: A genuine wicket-taking spinner (googly, doosra) vs a stopper (flat, economical). Very different threats.

items.q: Death bowling specialist — do they have one? (Overs 16–20)
items.d: A reliable death bowler restricts by 15–20 runs vs a weak unit. Single biggest T20 differentiator.

items.q: Economy rate and wicket profile for each main bowler
items.d: Economy 6 + 0 wickets = stopper. Economy 9 + 3 wickets = expensive but dangerous. Know the type.

items.q: All-rounders — who can bowl part-time overs if needed?
items.d: All-rounders give captains enormous flexibility. Identify them so bowling changes make sense to you.

items.q: Openers — aggressive or anchor types?
items.d: Repeat Team A analysis. Compare the two opening pairs — which powerplay battle do you expect to dominate?

items.q: Middle order (3–5) — can they handle pressure situations?
items.d: Batting at 3 wickets down in over 8 needs different skills than coming in at 30/0. Who fits each role?

items.q: Finishers — compare directly to Team A's finishers
items.d: This is often the decisive edge. A team with Hardik Pandya at 6 vs a team with a part-timer at 6 is huge.

items.q: Form of top 3 in last 5 innings
items.d: Same check as Team A. Cross-reference: is an in-form Team B batter facing an out-of-form Team A bowler?

items.q: Weaknesses vs specific bowling types
items.d: Does anyone struggle vs left-arm pace? Short pitch? If Team A has that bowler — expect them to target it.

items.q: Confirmed playing XI — any surprise inclusions?
items.d: A young debutant or unexpected inclusion changes the team dynamic entirely. Know before the toss.

items.q: Fast vs spin composition — compare to Team A
items.d: Which team's bowling attack is better suited to today's conditions? That team has a structural advantage.

items.q: Powerplay bowling options — wicket-takers or restrictors?
items.d: Wickets in powerplay = massive pressure. A team that contains but doesn't take wickets loses powerplay battles.

items.q: Middle-over spinners — quality of variation
items.d: Googly, carrom ball, arm ball — these are match-winning deliveries in the right conditions. Know who has them.

items.q: Death bowling — specialist or improvised?
items.d: Is the captain using a designated death bowler or cobbling together overs 18–20 with part-timers?

items.q: Economy and wickets for each main bowler (last 5 games)
items.d: A bowler with economy 8+ is being hit. A bowler with economy 6 but no wickets is a stopper. Know both.

items.q: Part-time bowling cover — who bails out the captain?
items.d: When a regular bowler is expensive, the captain has to get overs somewhere. Know who the backup is.

items.q: Pitch type — flat/batting, spin-friendly, or seaming/green?
items.d: Flat + dry = batting paradise. Green tinge = pace swing/seam. Dusty/worn = spinners grip more.

items.q: Average first innings score at this venue (last 10 matches)
items.d: This is your calibration number. 155 when average is 170 = underperformance. 185 = excellent total.

items.q: Batting first vs chasing record at this ground
items.d: Some grounds heavily favour chasing (dew, flat). Others reward batting first (early swing, deteriorating surface).

items.q: Does it historically suit spinners or pacers more?
items.d: Wankhede = pace, quick and bouncy. Chepauk = spin, slow and gripping. Knowing this tells you who has the edge.

items.q: How does pitch play differently in 2nd innings?
items.d: Most pitches get flatter as match progresses. But spin pitches get harder — rough develops outside off stump.

items.q: Day match or Day-Night (D/N)?
items.d: D/N almost always = dew in 2nd innings. Wet ball doesn't swing, grip or spin. Strongly favours chasing team.

items.q: Overcast or bright sunshine?
items.d: Overcast = swing bowling. Pacers get more air movement. Sunshine = dry conditions = better for spin later.

items.q: Hot & dry (40°C+) or cool and humid?
items.d: Extreme heat dries pitch faster, benefits spinners later. Cool damp preserves pitch and assists swing longer.

items.q: Rain risk? (DLS method awareness)
items.d: Rain mid-match = DLS revised target. Batting first team often disadvantaged under DLS. Know if it's in play.

items.q: Does the toss decision make sense given your research?
items.d: If you've done sections 1–6, the toss should feel logical or surprising. A surprise = something to figure out.

items.q: What is each team's likely gameplan today?
items.d: Form your own prediction. 'Team A will exploit spin.' 'Team B openers will attack.' Then verify during match.

items.q: What is today's par score for this pitch?
items.d: Combine venue average with conditions. Overcast + seaming? Adjust par down 10–15. This is your live benchmark.

phases.name: Phase 2 · During the Match
phases.title: Live Match Intelligence
phases.sub: Three phases within each innings. These are observation checkpoints — watch and verify in real time.

items.q: Powerplay score — Strong (50+), Average (35–50), Weak (<35)?
items.d: 50+ = batting dominated. Below 35 with 2+ wickets = bowling won powerplay. Sets tone for everything after.

items.q: Did the opening bowler swing or seam the ball early?
items.d: Beating the bat consistently = winning the battle even without wickets. Edges and misses are pressure, not luck.

items.q: Wickets in powerplay — how many and whose?
items.d: 0 wickets = batters won. 1 = roughly even. 2+ = bowling won. Each early wicket is worth more than one later.

items.q: Field placement — attacking or defensive? When did it switch?
items.d: Slips + gully = hunting wickets. Spread ring = restricting. Spot every switch — it reveals the captain's reading.

items.q: Are pre-match plans being followed? Any early surprises?
items.d: A spinner in over 2, unusual field, unexpected bowling change — the captain is reacting. Figure out why.

items.q: Are spinners building pressure? Count dot balls per over.
items.d: 3+ dot balls per over = pressure building. Batters under dot ball pressure make rash mistakes soon after.

items.q: Are batters rotating strike or getting bogged down?
items.d: 1s and 2s + occasional boundary = good batting. Someone on 10 off 15 in over 11 is in serious trouble.

items.q: Watch THE key battle: spinner vs set batter in the middle.
items.d: Is the spinner varying pace, flight, trajectory? Is the batter sweeping, stepping out, or staying back? This is chess.

items.q: Which partnership is building — and when does the captain act?
items.d: A 50+ partnership at run-a-ball in middle overs is a momentum shift. The bowling captain will try to break it.

items.q: At over 15 — is total tracking above or below your par score?
items.d: If par is 160 and they're 95/3 after 15, they need 65 off 5 = 13/over. Can their remaining batting manage that?

items.q: Is the death bowler executing yorkers or leaking wide full tosses?
items.d: A perfect yorker = hardest ball to hit. Wide full toss = free boundary. This battle determines final 20–30 runs.

items.q: Is the finisher still in? How many balls do they have?
items.d: Finisher arriving over 18 with 12 balls can score 30–40. Dismissed in over 16 = lower order must bat. Track this.

items.q: Final total — compare against your par score prediction.
items.d: Was your par accurate? If not, what did you miss? Every inaccuracy is a calibration lesson for next match.

items.q: Set the chase benchmark — what RRR does the chasing team face?
items.d: Target ÷ 20 = runs/over needed. Under 8 = comfortable. 8–10 = competitive. Above 10 = real pressure. Set expectation.

items.q: CRR vs RRR — is chasing team ahead of or behind required rate?
items.d: Current Run Rate above Required Run Rate = chasing team in control. Check this after every 5 overs minimum.

items.q: Watch for dew — is the ball skidding more than in 1st innings?
items.d: Bowlers wiping ball constantly, spinners going for more runs, deliveries not gripping = dew is a factor.

items.q: When does RRR cross 12? That is the crisis threshold.
items.d: RRR above 12 = serious danger. Above 15 = mathematically possible but practically nearly impossible.

items.q: Key wickets — who was dismissed, in which over, and how?
items.d: Each wicket in a chase simultaneously reduces batting quality AND increases required run rate. Note every one.

items.q: Who is the chase anchor — is one batter going the distance?
items.d: Successful chases almost always have one batter who bats from over 1–18+. Their wicket = match shifts dramatically.

items.q: Final over equation — runs needed vs wickets in hand.
items.d: 15 off last over with 2 wickets = very hard. 15 off last over with 6 wickets = very achievable. Both numbers matter.

phases.name: Phase 3 · Post-Match Debrief
phases.title: Post-Match Debrief
phases.sub: 15 minutes after the match. This is where casual viewers stop — and where real understanding begins.

items.q: What was THE single turning point of the match?
items.d: One over, one wicket, one dropped catch that changed momentum. Pundits always find this. Can you?

items.q: Which individual battle was most decisive?
items.d: Spinner vs set batter? Death bowler vs finisher? Opener vs new ball? Which matchup decided the outcome?

items.q: Was the toss decision correct? Was it decisive?
items.d: Did quality difference between teams outweigh conditions advantage? Or did the toss winner cruise?

items.q: How accurate were your pre-match predictions?
items.d: What did you get right? What surprised you? Every wrong prediction = something new to understand.

items.q: Were there captaincy errors by either side?
items.d: Wrong bowling change, batter promoted too late, poor field placement. Errors separate good teams from champions.

items.q: Player of the Match — do you agree? Who would you pick?
items.d: The formal award isn't always the most impactful player. A bowler's 2 wickets in over 14 may have decided it.

items.q: Who underperformed relative to their pre-match reputation?
items.d: Was a 'key bowler' taken apart? Did a star batter fall cheaply? What caused it — plan, conditions, form?

items.q: Did any lower-ranked or unexpected player have an outsized impact?
items.d: No. 8 scoring 25 off 10. Part-time spinner taking 2 key wickets. These are cricket's hidden stories.

items.q: Which bowler-batter matchups went as you predicted?
items.d: E.g. did the spinner exploit the weakness you identified pre-match? Confirming predictions builds real knowledge.

items.q: Update your mental model: one thing learned about each team.
items.d: 'Team A's death bowling is weaker than I thought.' 'Their No.3 is the real anchor, not the opener.' Write it.

items.q: Did the pitch play as you expected — batting, spin, or seam?
items.d: If spinners got less turn than expected, or the pitch stayed flatter longer — why? What did you miss?

items.q: Did dew factor in as predicted? How much did it affect bowling?
items.d: Compare 1st vs 2nd innings economy rates for spinners and pacers. The gap reveals how much dew mattered.

items.q: Was the par score your estimate was accurate?
items.d: If average is 170 and you predicted 158 due to conditions, but 182 was scored — recalibrate your model.

items.q: Which team's bowling attack was better suited to today's conditions?
items.d: In hindsight, did the pitch suit one attack more? Did the captain who won the toss make the right call?

items.q: Check dismissal types — caught, bowled, LBW, run out. Any pattern?
items.d: Multiple caught-behinds = swing/seam working. Multiple LBWs = spinner beating batters through the gate.

items.q: Economy rates by bowling phase — powerplay, middle, death
items.d: A bowler with economy 6 overall but 11 in death overs is excellent in overs 1–15 but dangerous at the end.

items.q: Dot ball % for each batter — who was truly in control?
items.d: A batter with 60% dot balls is under pressure even if they scored 40. A batter on 30% is dominating.

items.q: Run rate progression over-by-over — where did the scoring surge or stall?
items.d: Overlay both innings' over-by-over rates in your mind. Where were the spells of dominance for each side?

items.q: Watch 5 min of post-match analysis — do the pundits make sense now?
items.d: After this framework, 'middle-over collapse' and 'death bowling fell apart' should be completely obvious to you.

items.q: Can you explain why the winning team won in 2–3 sentences?
items.d: Not 'they scored more' — the real reason. 'Spinners took 4 wickets in overs 8–15 which strangled the chase.'

items.q: Identify one tactical decision that was genuinely clever.
items.d: E.g. bowling a part-timer before the main spinner to set up a matchup. Recognising smart tactics = advanced watching.

items.q: Identify one tactical decision that was a clear mistake.
items.d: Everyone makes mistakes — the key is knowing WHY it was wrong, not just that it failed. Outcome ≠ decision quality.

items.q: Write down one specific thing to research before the next match.
items.d: E.g. 'Understand reverse swing.' 'What is the rough and why do spinners target it?' Carry the learning forward.

items.q: Share one observation in a cricket discussion. Can you hold the conversation?
items.d: The true test of understanding is articulating it to others. One technical insight per match = rapid improvement.
